learning_objectives: Describe the structure and types of neurons and glial cells
Explain the electrochemical basis of the action potential
Describe synaptic transmission including neurotransmitter release, binding, and termination

lecture_notes.introduction: Biological psychology bridges neuroscience and behavior. Every thought, feeling, and action depends on neural communication, and understanding these mechanisms provides the biological foundation for all of psychology.
key_concepts: Neuron structure: dendrites, soma, axon hillock, axon, terminal buttons
Resting potential (-70mV), depolarization, action potential propagation
Saltatory conduction along myelinated axons
Ionotropic vs. metabotropic receptors
Neurotransmitter systems: glutamate, GABA, dopamine, serotonin, norepinephrine, acetylcholine
lecture_notes.demonstration: Computer simulation of action potential generation and propagation with manipulable parameters

review.value: All-or-none electrical signal propagated along the axon; initiated when depolarization reaches threshold (~-55mV)
Neurotransmitter release from vesicles into synaptic cleft, binding to postsynaptic receptors, terminated by reuptake or enzymatic degradation

class_schedule.topic: Brain Anatomy and Methods of Investigation
learning_objectives: Identify major brain structures and their functions
Compare structural and functional neuroimaging methods
Describe lesion, stimulation, and recording methods in neuroscience

lecture_notes.introduction: Understanding brain anatomy and research methods is essential for interpreting the neuroscience literature that informs biological psychology.
key_concepts: Hindbrain: medulla, pons, cerebellum
Midbrain: superior and inferior colliculi, substantia nigra
Forebrain: thalamus, hypothalamus, hippocampus, amygdala, basal ganglia, cerebral cortex
Structural imaging: CT, MRI; functional imaging: PET, fMRI, EEG, MEG
Lesion studies, deep brain stimulation, TMS, optogenetics
lecture_notes.demonstration: Virtual brain dissection using 3D neuroanatomy software: label structures and predict functional deficits from lesions

review.value: Measures blood oxygenation (BOLD signal) as a proxy for neural activity; excellent spatial resolution, poor temporal resolution
If lesion A impairs function X but not Y, and lesion B impairs Y but not X, then X and Y depend on different neural substrates

learning_objectives: Explain how agonists and antagonists affect neurotransmitter systems
Describe the mechanisms of major drug classes: stimulants, depressants, opioids, psychedelics
Discuss the neuroscience of addiction and tolerance
readings: Kalat, Ch. 3 (drug section), supplementary reading on addiction neuroscience

lecture_notes.introduction: Psychopharmacology reveals how drugs modify neural communication and behavior, providing both therapeutic tools and models for understanding normal brain function.
key_concepts: Agonists enhance and antagonists block neurotransmitter activity
Cocaine blocks dopamine reuptake; amphetamine reverses the transporter
Alcohol potentiates GABA and inhibits glutamate (NMDA) receptors
Opioids bind mu receptors in pain and reward circuits
Addiction: mesolimbic dopamine, incentive salience, neuroplasticity in prefrontal-striatal circuits
lecture_notes.demonstration: Diagram the mechanism of action for four drug classes at the synapse: cocaine, alcohol, heroin, LSD

review.value: VTA to nucleus accumbens projection; activated by all drugs of abuse; mediates incentive salience (wanting) not hedonia (liking)
Decreased drug effect with repeated use; involves receptor downregulation, metabolic changes, and conditioned compensatory responses

class_schedule.topic: Sleep, Circadian Rhythms, and Biological Clocks
learning_objectives: Describe the neural mechanisms controlling circadian rhythms
Explain the stages of sleep and their neural bases
Discuss the functions of sleep based on current research

lecture_notes.introduction: Sleep occupies one-third of our lives. Understanding its neural regulation and functions is critical for biological psychology and has major implications for health and cognition.
key_concepts: Suprachiasmatic nucleus (SCN) as the master clock
Melatonin secretion by the pineal gland
Sleep stages: NREM (N1-N3) and REM; associated EEG patterns
Ascending reticular activating system and sleep-wake regulation
Functions of sleep: memory consolidation, synaptic homeostasis hypothesis, immune function
lecture_notes.demonstration: EEG recording analysis: identify sleep stages from sample polysomnography tracings

review.value: Hypothalamic structure that generates circadian rhythms; entrained by light via the retinohypothalamic tract
Tononi and Cirelli: waking strengthens synapses; sleep globally downscales synaptic strength to maintain capacity for new learning

learning_objectives: Describe the role of the amygdala in fear conditioning and emotion
Explain the HPA axis and the stress response
Discuss the neuroscience of anxiety, aggression, and reward

lecture_notes.introduction: Emotion is not the opposite of reason but an essential neural system for adaptive behavior. This session explores the brain circuits underlying emotional experience and the stress response.
key_concepts: Amygdala: fear conditioning, threat detection, emotional memory
LeDoux's dual-pathway model: thalamus-amygdala (fast) vs. thalamus-cortex-amygdala (slow)
HPA axis: hypothalamus (CRH) -> pituitary (ACTH) -> adrenal cortex (cortisol)
Chronic stress: cortisol-mediated hippocampal atrophy and prefrontal dysfunction
Prefrontal regulation of amygdala: top-down emotion regulation
lecture_notes.demonstration: Trace the neural pathway of fear conditioning from tone-shock pairing to conditioned fear response

review.value: Central hub for threat detection and emotional memory; receives rapid subcortical input and modulates memory consolidation via hippocampus
Hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis; cortisol release supports acute stress adaptation but chronic activation damages hippocampus and immune function

learning_objectives: Explain long-term potentiation as a cellular mechanism of learning
Describe the roles of the hippocampus and cortex in memory consolidation
Discuss neural plasticity across development and in response to experience

lecture_notes.introduction: Memory is not stored in a single location but distributed across neural circuits. Understanding the cellular and systems-level mechanisms of memory is a central achievement of biological psychology.
key_concepts: Hebb's rule: neurons that fire together wire together
Long-term potentiation (LTP): NMDA receptor, calcium influx, AMPA receptor insertion
Hippocampal role in episodic memory formation and spatial navigation
Systems consolidation: hippocampus to neocortex transfer during sleep
Neurogenesis in the adult hippocampus and its functional significance
lecture_notes.demonstration: Diagram the molecular steps of LTP induction at a glutamatergic synapse

review.value: Persistent strengthening of synapses based on recent patterns of activity; requires NMDA receptor activation and postsynaptic calcium influx
Bilateral medial temporal lobe removal abolished new episodic memory formation but spared procedural memory, demonstrating multiple memory systems

questions.question: Explain why cocaine and amphetamine both increase dopamine signaling but through different synaptic mechanisms. Describe the mechanism of each drug at the synapse.
questions.answer: Cocaine blocks the dopamine transporter (DAT), preventing reuptake of dopamine from the synaptic cleft. Dopamine accumulates in the cleft and continues to stimulate postsynaptic receptors. Amphetamine also blocks DAT but additionally reverses the transporter, causing it to pump dopamine OUT of the presynaptic terminal into the cleft. Amphetamine also enters vesicles and displaces stored dopamine. Both increase extracellular dopamine, but amphetamine produces a larger release because it actively pushes dopamine out rather than simply blocking recycling.

rubric.key: Correct amphetamine mechanism including transporter reversal
Explains why amphetamine produces greater dopamine increase

questions.question: Describe how an inhibitory postsynaptic potential (IPSP) is generated and explain why IPSPs are important for normal brain function.
questions.answer: An IPSP is generated when an inhibitory neurotransmitter (typically GABA) binds to receptors that open chloride channels or potassium channels. Chloride influx or potassium efflux hyperpolarizes the membrane, moving it further from threshold and making an action potential less likely. IPSPs are essential for normal brain function because they provide balance to excitatory activity. Without inhibition, excitation would spread unchecked, leading to seizures (as seen when GABA is blocked). IPSPs also sharpen neural coding, enable temporal precision, and support oscillatory patterns important for cognition.

rubric.key: Identifies GABA and ion channel mechanisms

questions.question: Describe LeDoux's dual-pathway model of fear processing and explain the adaptive value of having both a fast and a slow route.
questions.answer: The fast (low road) pathway: sensory information goes from the thalamus directly to the amygdala, bypassing the cortex. This produces a rapid, crude fear response (e.g., jumping at a stick that might be a snake). The slow (high road) pathway: information goes from the thalamus to the sensory cortex for detailed processing, then to the amygdala. This allows a more accurate evaluation (it's just a stick). Adaptive value: the fast route enables immediate defensive responses to potential threats before conscious recognition, maximizing survival. The slow route prevents unnecessary fear responses by providing accurate threat assessment.

rubric.key: Explains adaptive value of dual system

questions.question: Describe the molecular mechanism of long-term potentiation (LTP) induction at a glutamatergic synapse, starting from presynaptic activity.
questions.answer: 1) High-frequency presynaptic stimulation releases glutamate. 2) Glutamate binds to both AMPA and NMDA receptors on the postsynaptic neuron. 3) AMPA receptor activation depolarizes the postsynaptic membrane. 4) Sufficient depolarization expels the Mg2+ block from the NMDA receptor channel. 5) With the Mg2+ block removed, the NMDA receptor opens to allow Ca2+ influx. 6) Calcium activates intracellular signaling cascades (CaMKII, PKC). 7) These kinases trigger insertion of additional AMPA receptors into the postsynaptic membrane. 8) The synapse is now strengthened: future glutamate release produces a larger postsynaptic response.

rubric.key: Describes NMDA receptor as coincidence detector (pre and post activity required)
Explains Mg2+ block removal and Ca2+ entry

major_assignments.title: Neuroscience of a Psychological Phenomenon
major_assignments.description: Choose a psychological phenomenon (e.g., addiction, PTSD, chronic pain, bilingualism, meditation) and write a comprehensive review of its neural basis. Integrate evidence from neuroimaging, lesion studies, pharmacology, and animal models to build a mechanistic account.
objectives: Synthesize neuroscience evidence from multiple methodologies
Build a coherent mechanistic account of a psychological phenomenon
Critically evaluate the strengths and limitations of different neuroscience methods
deliverables: Neural circuit diagram for the phenomenon
Critical evaluation of methodological strengths and limitations

criteria: Integrates multiple levels of analysis (molecular, cellular, systems)
Evaluates strengths and limitations of evidence
Distinguishes correlation from causation in neuroimaging
Neural circuit diagram is accurate and informative
Comprehensive and accurate neuroscience content integrating multiple methodologies
Thoughtful critical analysis of evidence quality
Coherent mechanistic account with informative neural circuit diagram
Clear, professional writing in APA format

weekly_quizzes.description: Regular assessment of neuroscience concepts and mechanisms

lab_assignments.description: Neuroanatomy, EEG analysis, and brain imaging interpretation exercises

midterm_examination.description: Exam covering neural communication through psychopharmacology

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam covering all biological psychology topics

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for all lab sessions involving neuroanatomy and data analysis. Lecture attendance is strongly recommended.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments are penalized 10% per day. Lab reports must be submitted by the next lab session.
course_policies.collaboration: Lab exercises may be completed in pairs. All written assignments and exams must be completed independently.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All work must be original with proper citations. Fabrication of data or lab results will result in course failure.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Access to neuroanatomy software, brain imaging viewers, and university library databases.
